reasoning: The average number of ACLED-recorded protests in the 360 days preceding the forecast date is exactly 0.000, meaning there were zero protests in the past year. Sao Tome and Principe is a very small island nation (~220,000 people) that historically registers exceedingly few protests, mostly limited to rare political or labor disputes which frequently take the form of stay-at-home strikes rather than public demonstrations (the latter being required for an ACLED 'Protest'). Extensive targeted searches in both English and Portuguese find no ongoing or recently planned protests in October or early November 2025. Additionally, the next scheduled presidential and legislative elections are not due until late 2026, meaning political instability strictly associated with an upcoming election cycle is unlikely in the windows leading up to February 2026. Therefore, predicting whether at least 1 protest will occur relies almost entirely on the base rate, which is less than 5% for any given 30-day window. Uncertainty increases slightly for the more distant dates, but given the extremely stable local environment, probabilities remain near the lower bound.
